votes.reason: The Python specification constructs the exact list of primes less than n in strictly ascending order, which uniquely satisfies all the conditions of the Coq specification.

The Python spec constructs the unique list of primes less than n in ascending order and compares it to the result, which exactly matches the Coq specification's requirements of primality, bound, completeness, sortedness, and uniqueness.

The Python code correctly implements the primality test and constructs the expected list of primes less than n in strictly ascending order, which uniquely satisfies all the conditions of the Coq specification.

The Python code generates the expected list of all primes less than n in strictly ascending order, which is the unique list satisfying all five Coq spec properties (all elements prime, all elements < n, all primes < n included, strictly sorted, no duplicates). The is_prime function correctly identifies primes, and comparing result == expected correctly checks whether result satisfies the Coq specification.

The Python code generates the expected list of all primes less than n in strictly ascending order, which is the unique list satisfying all five Coq spec properties (all elements prime, all elements < n, all primes < n included, strictly sorted, no duplicates). The is_prime function correctly identifies primes, and comparing result == expected correctly checks whether result satisfies the Coq specification.

The Python spec constructs the unique sorted list of primes less than n and checks equality. The Coq spec requires all elements are prime, all are less than n, all primes less than n are included, the list is strictly sorted, and has no duplicates. These five properties together uniquely determine the same list that the Python code constructs. So for all concrete inputs, the Python check returns True if and only if the Coq spec is satisfied.

Python builds the strictly increasing list of all primes < n and checks equality with result, which matches the Coq spec’s requirements (all and only primes < n, sorted, no duplicates). The primality test is correct for natural numbers, so behavior aligns for all n >= 0.

Python computes the strictly increasing list of all primes less than n and checks equality with result, which matches the Coq spec’s completeness, bounds, primality, order, and no-duplicates properties for all n >= 0.
Python computes the sorted list of all primes < n and checks equality. Its primality test matches Coq’s prime on natural numbers, so for all n >= 0 the results coincide.
